title: Calculating handspun yardage
subtitle: How to measure the length of a handspun skein and plan yarn quantity for a project
excerpt: A handspun skein has weight and length. Knowing the yards per gram (or metres per gram) of a sample skein lets you calculate how much fibre to spin for a particular project. This guide explains how to measure the length of a skein on a niddy-noddy, calculate the wraps per inch, and use those numbers to plan spinning projects.
sourceNotes: Yarn measurement and planning methods are standard guild practice. Wraps-per-inch measurement is documented across modern spinning guides including Judith MacKenzie McCuin's work (in copyright, not quoted). Method described is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A measurement of yarn thickness: the number of times a yarn wraps around a ruler or stick across one inch without overlapping. More wraps per inch means a finer yarn.
A yarn measurement expressing how many yards of yarn are in each gram of weight. It is calculated by measuring a skein's total length and dividing by the skein's weight.

content.text: Two numbers describe a handspun yarn: its
(WPI), which indicates the yarn weight category, and its
(YPG), which tells you how much fibre to spin for a given project. Knowing both lets you match handspun yarn to patterns written for commercial yarn weights.
Measuring the skein on a niddy-noddy

A standard niddy-noddy produces a skein of known circumference: a 45 cm niddy-noddy produces loops 90 cm long (around the two ends and back). Count the number of loops in the skein when winding it off. Multiply by the circumference to get total length.
Example: 60 loops on a 45 cm niddy-noddy = 60 x 90 cm = 5,400 cm = 54 metres = approximately 59 yards.

Weigh the skein on a digital scale. Divide total length by weight to get YPG. A lace-weight skein of 20 g and 200 m produces 10 m per gram (or about 11 yards per gram). A worsted-weight skein of 50 g and 100 m produces 2 m per gram.

Wind a length of yarn around a ruler or pencil, wrapping side by side without overlapping and without stretching the yarn. Count the number of wraps that fit in one inch (2.5 cm). The WPI table for handspun yarn generally follows this scale:
30+ WPI: lace weight. 22-30 WPI: fingering or sock weight. 16-22 WPI: sport weight. 12-16 WPI: DK or worsted. 8-12 WPI: aran or bulky. Under 8 WPI: super bulky.

A knitting or weaving pattern lists the yarn weight and total yardage needed. Match your handspun's WPI to the required yarn weight. Then calculate how many grams of roving to spin to reach the required yardage, using your measured YPG. Spin 10-15% more than calculated to allow for swatch waste and any differences in finishing shrinkage.

If spinning for a weaving project, calculate total warp length from the project width, sett (ends per cm), and the loom waste allowance. Add warp take-up (typically 10-15% for cotton, 20-25% for wool, depending on sett).

Record the fibre weight, yield (grams of yarn), total length, WPI, and any notes on twist and finish for each skein. Over time, the log becomes a reference for how a particular fibre performs on your wheel. A log entry for one successful skein makes planning the next project with the same fibre straightforward.
